rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical intermolecular [2+2] cycloaddition between an enol ether (1-(prop-1-en-1-yloxy)-4-propylbenzene) and an olefinic carboxylic acid (hex-5-enoic acid) via an anodically generated radical cation, with the olefin nucleophile noncovalently condensed at the electrode surface.
electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the electron-rich aryl enol ether (1) to its radical cation, which is trapped at the electrode surface by the surface-condensed olefin (2) to give a [2+2] cycloadduct.

key_intermediates.name: aryl enol ether radical cation of 1-(prop-1-en-1-yloxy)-4-propylbenzene
distonic radical cation / 1,4-radical cation intermediate from C-C bond formation with hex-5-enoic acid

electron_flow: The anode removes one electron from the electron-rich aryl enol ether 1 to give a short-lived radical cation; the pendant olefin of hex-5-enoic acid 2, noncovalently condensed at the electrode surface, traps this radical cation and ring-closes to the cyclobutane radical cation, which is reduced back to the neutral [2+2] adduct 3.
rate_determining_step: trapping of the transient radical cation of 1 by the olefin nucleophile 2 (limited by its local concentration at the electrode surface)

probe_reagents.name: high-equivalent loading of hex-5-enoic acid (20 mol equiv., 200 mM)
probe_reagents.observation: Required excess for effective trapping of the short-lived radical cation under conventional conditions; yields of 3 dropped rapidly as the olefin amount was decreased, whereas with surface-condensed electrodes high yields were retained even with much less olefin.

notes: Mechanism is presented as a proposal supported indirectly by the dependence of yield on olefin loading and by the comparison between conventional vs surface-condensed electrode conditions; no TEMPO/BHT trapping, CV, EPR, or DFT data shown on the provided page. Only the first page of the communication was supplied, so later mechanistic details (if any) are not captured.
